heroOverview: Hypochondriasis (ICD-10: F45.21) is a persistent preoccupation with the fear of having, or the belief that one has, a serious illness, persisting despite adequate medical evaluation and reassurance. Epidemiological surveys consistently place lifetime prevalence at 1–5% of the general population, and the condition accounts for a disproportionate share of unnecessary medical consultations and investigations. The central feature is not the physical symptoms themselves but the catastrophic interpretation of normal body sensations or minor signs as evidence of grave disease. Although formal diagnosis requires at least six months of symptoms, many people carry the disorder for years before a psychiatric explanation is offered. Effective psychological treatments, particularly cognitive-behavioural therapy, achieve clinically significant improvement in the majority of patients who complete a full course.

keyStats.prevalence: Lifetime prevalence 1–5% of the general population; up to 9% of primary-care outpatients meet criteria (Fink et al., Am J Psychiatry 2004)
keyStats.demographics: Affects men and women roughly equally; peak onset in early adulthood (20s–30s), with a second peak in the 50s
keyStats.avgAge: Onset most commonly in early adulthood; chronic course typical without treatment
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated several tens of millions of people worldwide living with clinically significant health anxiety at any time

definition: Hypochondriasis (ICD-10: F45.21) is classified under somatoform disorders and is characterised by a persistent and distressing preoccupation with the possibility of having one or more serious progressive physical illnesses. The conviction is maintained despite normal physical examination findings and appropriate medical investigation and reassurance. The person may focus on specific organ systems, cycling among cancers, cardiac disease, neurological conditions, or other feared diagnoses over time. The disorder is distinguished from delusional disorder in that most people retain partial insight — they can, at some level, acknowledge that the fear may be exaggerated — but that acknowledgement provides little sustained relief. Duration of at least six months is required for the ICD-10 diagnosis. Functionally, hypochondriasis is associated with reduced quality of life comparable to depression, high rates of medical utilisation, and significant occupational impairment. It falls within the psychiatry specialty and is related to obsessive-compulsive spectrum and anxiety spectrum disorders; DSM-5 has reorganised the construct into illness anxiety disorder (minimal somatic symptoms) and somatic symptom disorder (prominent somatic symptoms), but the ICD-10 category remains in active clinical use.

typesClassification.description: The most common presentation: repeated clinic visits, internet searches, or demands for investigations to seek reassurance, followed by temporary relief that quickly gives way to renewed anxiety.

Instead of seeking reassurance, the person avoids medical settings, health news, or any perceived exposure to illness, leading to significant functional restriction and delayed detection of genuine health problems.
The preoccupation is anchored in real but amplified bodily sensations — minor palpitations, headaches, or bowel changes — that are perceived as catastrophic evidence of disease.
A specific, persistent fear of contracting a particular disease (classically cancer or HIV), in the absence of significant somatic symptoms, driving repeated screening and checking behaviours.

primarySymptoms: Persistent preoccupation with the fear of having a serious illness lasting six months or more, returning even after medical reassurance and normal test results.
Misinterpretation of normal body sensations — heartbeat awareness, muscle twitches, minor aches — as certain signs of cancer, heart disease, or neurological illness.
Excessive health-related behaviours including repeated clinic visits, frequent self-examination (checking lumps, pulse, skin), and extensive internet symptom searching.
Reassurance-seeking from doctors, family, and friends, obtaining brief relief followed by rapid return of doubt and renewed anxiety.
High health-related anxiety and distress that is disproportionate to the actual likelihood of illness based on objective medical findings.
Avoidance of situations perceived as health-threatening, such as hospitals, news articles about illness, or conversations about disease.
Preoccupation with bodily functions — monitoring bowel habits, urine colour, skin texture, or heart rate — beyond what medical management requires.
Functional impairment in work, social relationships, and daily activities caused by health-focused thinking occupying several hours a day.
Difficulty accepting normal medical reassurance, with repeated requests for additional tests or second opinions after normal results.
Heightened awareness of and attention to normal physiological variation, such as asymmetries, minor bruises, or moles, leading to catastrophic interpretation.
earlyWarningSigns: Escalating internet searches about symptoms after a minor health scare that do not stop when results are normal.
Repeatedly checking a body part for a suspected abnormality more than once a day.
An inability to feel relief after a doctor says nothing is wrong, with doubts resurfacing within hours.
Avoiding films, articles, or conversations about illness because they trigger intense fear.
emergencySigns: Suicidal ideation or self-harm driven by health-related despair — seek emergency mental health services immediately.
Complete functional paralysis: unable to work, leave home, or maintain daily activities because of health fear.
Sudden onset of extremely high-intensity health panic accompanied by derealization, dissociation, or inability to manage basic self-care.
New or rapidly escalating symptoms in a person with known hypochondriasis that have not been evaluated — always exclude genuine illness first.

causes.description: People with hypochondriasis apply a systematic attentional bias toward internal body signals and interpret normal physiological variation — heartbeat, gut sounds, muscle twitches — as evidence of disease, generating anxiety that amplifies the perceived intensity of those sensations in a self-reinforcing cycle.

A personal history of serious illness, or exposure to severe illness or death in a parent or close relative, especially during childhood, primes heightened vigilance toward body signals and establishes mental schemas linking symptoms with dangerous outcomes.

Each round of seeking and obtaining reassurance provides short-term anxiety relief, but this relief reinforces checking behaviour and prevents extinction of the underlying fear, maintaining the disorder over time.

Growing up in a household where illness was associated with special attention, care, or permission to avoid aversive activities, or conversely where illness was treated as catastrophic, shapes adult beliefs about bodily sensations and their significance.

Neuroimaging and psychophysiological studies suggest heightened insula and anterior cingulate activity in health anxiety, reflecting amplified interoceptive processing and difficulty modulating the threat response to ambiguous internal stimuli.

Acute stressors — bereavement, job loss, relationship breakdown — often precipitate or exacerbate hypochondriasis by reducing general resilience, increasing background arousal, and focusing attention inward toward physical states.

riskFactors.description: Having experienced a genuine serious illness, or a false alarm that generated significant medical workup, establishes conditioned fear responses and illness-focused schemas that persist after recovery.
First-degree relatives of people with hypochondriasis or OCD have elevated rates of the same disorders, reflecting shared heritable anxiety sensitivity and threat-appraisal style.
Parental overprotection around illness, frequent medical visits for minor complaints in childhood, or exposure to a seriously ill parent increases the risk of health anxiety in adulthood.
Frequent exposure to disease-focused online content, medical dramas, or detailed symptom databases ('cyberchondria') fuels reassurance-seeking cycles and maintains preoccupation.
Generalised anxiety disorder, OCD, panic disorder, and major depression are present in 60–80% of people with hypochondriasis, reflecting shared vulnerability and mutual maintenance.

riskFactors.factor: Low health literacy with high catastrophising tendency
riskFactors.description: People who lack a clear framework for understanding normal physiological variation but who habitually assume worst-case explanations are particularly prone to health-fear escalation.
Losing a close person to sudden or unexpected illness — particularly from a condition that went undetected — is a recognised precipitant for hypochondriasis onset in susceptible individuals.

affectedDemographics: Adults in their 20s and 30s presenting to primary care with repeated unsubstantiated health concerns
Men and women in roughly equal proportions, unlike most anxiety disorders
Older adults in their 50s, in whom a second incidence peak coincides with genuine age-related health changes
People with a history of serious personal or family illness
Individuals with co-occurring generalised anxiety disorder, OCD, or major depression
High internet users with frequent engagement with medical information sites
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of hypochondriasis is clinical, based on the history and mental state examination, and requires ruling out genuine underlying medical illness as well as other psychiatric explanations. The cornerstone of assessment is establishing that health preoccupation has persisted for at least six months, causes significant distress or functional impairment, and continues despite appropriate medical investigation and reassurance. Clinicians use structured interviews and validated instruments — the Health Anxiety Inventory (HAI) and the Illness Attitude Scales are the most widely used — to quantify severity. Medical evaluation must be proportionate and targeted: a person who reports years of recurring 'cancer fear' without any objective signs does not warrant further oncological workup beyond standard age-appropriate screening, because repeated investigation reinforces rather than relieves the disorder. Key psychiatric differentials include generalised anxiety disorder (worry is not limited to health), OCD (obsessive thoughts and compulsive checking overlap substantially), panic disorder (episodic rather than persistent health fear), depression with somatic preoccupation, delusional disorder (where illness conviction lacks insight and resists all persuasion), and genuine medical conditions. The clinical interview should explore illness beliefs, checking and reassurance-seeking behaviours, childhood health experiences, and family patterns around illness. Neurological or endocrine conditions — hyperthyroidism, cardiac arrhythmias — that produce genuine somatic symptoms should be excluded with basic investigations.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A 14- or 18-item validated questionnaire that quantifies health anxiety severity and guides treatment planning and monitoring.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A brief self-report form completed in about 5 minutes; scores above a threshold suggest clinically significant health anxiety warranting further assessment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes DSM/ICD diagnoses systematically, distinguishing hypochondriasis from OCD, GAD, panic disorder, and depression.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician asks a series of standardised questions about symptoms, duration, and functional impact over 30–60 minutes; results guide the formal diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures illness fears, disease conviction, bodily checking, and reassurance-seeking behaviours to profile the specific maintaining factors in each patient.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A paper or digital questionnaire of about 27 items; responses inform the formulation used in cognitive-behavioural therapy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Proportionate physical examination and selected investigations to exclude genuine medical conditions that could explain symptoms, performed once and not repeated without new clinical indication.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard examination and blood or imaging tests selected according to symptoms; results that are normal are communicated clearly alongside psychoeducation about the disorder.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rules out hyperthyroidism, anaemia, and other endocrine or metabolic causes of anxiety, palpitations, and fatigue that can anchor health preoccupation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw; results available within 24 hours.

treatmentOverview: Cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT) is the first-line evidence-based treatment for hypochondriasis, with randomised controlled trials consistently showing response rates of 50–65% with clinically meaningful reduction in health anxiety maintained at one-year follow-up. The key components of CBT for health anxiety are: (1) psychoeducation about the cognitive model — how attention, interpretation, and safety behaviours maintain fear rather than resolve it; (2) cognitive restructuring to develop more accurate, proportionate illness beliefs; (3) behavioural experiments to test feared outcomes and reduce checking and reassurance-seeking; and (4) exposure with response prevention, directly analogous to OCD treatment. Pharmacological treatment with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) has good evidence: fluoxetine, paroxetine, and fluvoxamine have all shown statistically and clinically significant benefits in randomised trials, with response rates of 60–70% in some studies. SSRIs are particularly useful for patients with comorbid depression or OCD features, and for those awaiting or unable to access CBT. A combination of CBT and pharmacotherapy is used for severe or treatment-resistant cases. The treatment philosophy emphasises that medical reassurance alone maintains the disorder by temporarily relieving anxiety without addressing the underlying cognitive and behavioural patterns; clinicians in primary care are therefore guided to limit reassurance-giving and redirect patients toward psychological treatment. Stepped care models — starting with self-help CBT materials, then therapist-guided CBT, then combined treatment — are supported by NICE guidelines and allow efficient use of limited mental health resources.

medicalTreatments.name: Cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT)
medicalTreatments.description: The gold-standard psychological treatment comprising psychoeducation, cognitive restructuring of illness beliefs, behavioural experiments reducing checking and reassurance-seeking, and exposure to feared illness-related stimuli. Delivered typically in 12–16 weekly sessions individually or in group format; internet-delivered CBT is also effective.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 50–65% of patients achieve clinically significant improvement; gains are maintained at 1-year follow-up in controlled trials (NICE CG91, 2009).

medicalTreatments.name: SSRIs (fluoxetine, paroxetine, escitalopram)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line pharmacotherapy for hypochondriasis, particularly when comorbid depression or OCD is present; they reduce health anxiety through serotonergic modulation of threat appraisal and are prescribed at standard anxiety-disorder doses for a minimum of 12 weeks before response is assessed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Randomised trials report response rates of 60–70% versus 30% on placebo for paroxetine; effect maintained at 12-month follow-up in naturalistic studies.

medicalTreatments.name: SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine)
medicalTreatments.description: Used as an alternative to SSRIs when the first-line agent is not tolerated or has failed; dual serotonin–noradrenaline reuptake inhibition may address both anxiety and somatic symptom amplification.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical evidence is less robust than for SSRIs but consistent with 40–55% response in clinical series; improvement in somatic complaints seen in 8–12 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT)
medicalTreatments.description: A third-wave behavioural therapy that targets experiential avoidance and fusion with health-fear thoughts, teaching psychological flexibility rather than symptom reduction as the primary goal; delivered in 8–12 sessions and increasingly evaluated in randomised trials for health anxiety.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analyses of ACT for health anxiety report effect sizes comparable to CBT (approximately 0.7–0.9 standard deviation reduction in HAI scores).

medicalTreatments.name: Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT)
medicalTreatments.description: Combines mindfulness meditation with CBT principles to develop a detached, observational relationship with health-fear thoughts; reduces rumination and symptom catastrophisation and is available in structured 8-week group programmes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Controlled studies show approximately 40–55% response rate in health anxiety; effect sustained at 6-month follow-up.

alternativeTreatments.name: Bibliotherapy (self-help CBT books)
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured self-help workbooks based on CBT principles for health anxiety, such as Overcoming Health Anxiety by Willson and Veale, have been evaluated in randomised trials and are an effective first step in stepped-care pathways, reaching patients who cannot immediately access therapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mindfulness meditation
alternativeTreatments.description: Regular mindfulness practice — 20–30 minutes daily — trains attentional flexibility and reduces automatic catastrophic interpretation of body sensations; most evidence supports it as an adjunct to formal therapy rather than a standalone treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Psychoeducation groups
alternativeTreatments.description: Brief structured group programmes explaining the cognitive maintenance model of health anxiety have been shown to reduce health-care utilisation and improve distress in primary-care populations without formal psychotherapy.

whySeeSpecialist: A psychiatrist or clinical psychologist with experience in health anxiety or OCD-spectrum disorders should be involved when health anxiety has persisted for more than six months, is severely impairing, or has not responded to a first antidepressant or brief psychoeducation in primary care. Specialist assessment distinguishes hypochondriasis from OCD, delusional disorder, somatic symptom disorder, and genuine medical conditions, and can initiate evidence-based CBT or combined treatment. Earlier specialist referral avoids years of expensive and counterproductive medical investigations.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a psychiatrist or clinical psychologist with specific expertise in health anxiety or OCD-spectrum conditions and demonstrable experience delivering CBT for somatic preoccupation. Ask whether the practice uses validated outcome measures such as the HAI to track progress. A clinician who collaborates with primary care physicians to limit counterproductive reassurance is particularly valuable. NICE-accredited services and academic health-anxiety clinics attached to research universities often have the greatest depth of expertise.
preventionStrategies: Early psychoeducation after a significant illness episode or medical scare, explaining the cognitive mechanisms of health anxiety before checking and reassurance patterns become entrenched.
Limiting repetitive symptom-searching online; setting clear boundaries on time spent on health-information sites reduces the cyberchondria cycle.
Building general anxiety resilience through regular physical exercise, sleep hygiene, and stress management before illness-specific fears consolidate.
Clinicians in primary care proactively recognising repeated attendance without clinical findings and offering psychological referral early rather than after years of investigation.
Psychoeducation for parents about normalising minor childhood illness without catastrophising, reducing illness-focused learning in susceptible children.
lifestyleModifications: Establish a firm daily limit on internet health searches — for example, no more than 10 minutes once a day on health topics.
Practice postponing checking behaviours (checking a mole, pulse, or lump) until a scheduled time rather than immediately, then gradually extending the interval.
Exercise regularly; aerobic activity for 30 minutes most days is associated with a clinically meaningful reduction in anxiety sensitivity.
Maintain a consistent sleep schedule, as sleep deprivation amplifies threat appraisal and body-sensation awareness.
Journal health-fear episodes to identify patterns, triggers, and the sequence of thoughts, feelings, and behaviours that maintain anxiety.
Reduce reassurance-seeking conversations with family and friends, explaining to them that reassurance, though kind, maintains the anxiety cycle.

recommended: Regular balanced meals to stabilise blood glucose and prevent the physical sensations (shakiness, palpitations) that can trigger misinterpretation
Adequate hydration; dehydration causes headaches and fatigue that may fuel symptom-monitoring
Foods rich in omega-3 fatty acids, which have moderate evidence supporting mood and anxiety stabilisation
avoid: Excessive caffeine, which increases heart rate, palpitations, and arousal, providing additional body sensations to catastrophise
Alcohol as a coping strategy; while it reduces immediate anxiety, it increases rebound anxiety the following day and worsens long-term outcomes
Energy drinks with high stimulant content for the same reasons as caffeine

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise — at minimum 150 minutes of moderate-intensity activity per week per WHO guidelines — is supported by controlled evidence as an effective adjunct for anxiety disorders including health anxiety. Exercise reduces background physiological arousal, improves mood, and provides a controlled context for experiencing elevated heart rate and other somatic sensations without catastrophe, which constitutes a form of interoceptive exposure. Patients are encouraged to exercise in a way that involves physical effort rather than avoiding any activity out of fear it might reveal or provoke illness.
dailyManagement: Use a brief structured anxiety check-in twice daily to note health-fear level on a 0–10 scale without ruminating or checking; this builds awareness without reinforcing preoccupation.
Apply a 'worry postponement' rule: when health thoughts arise outside the check-in, note them and defer engagement to a designated 15-minute worry period.
Complete CBT homework assignments consistently between sessions; research shows that out-of-session practice predicts outcome more strongly than session frequency alone.
Communicate clearly with your primary-care doctor about the diagnosis so the team can coordinate limited, proportionate medical responses.
Identify and reduce safety behaviours one by one: each week, choose one checking behaviour to delay or eliminate, recording the anxiety before and after.
prognosis: Without treatment, hypochondriasis tends to follow a chronic, waxing-and-waning course: population studies show that approximately 50–70% of untreated patients still meet criteria at five-year follow-up, and the disorder is associated with sustained impairment in social, occupational, and physical functioning. With evidence-based treatment — CBT, pharmacotherapy, or both — the outlook is substantially more favourable. Systematic reviews report that 50–65% of patients who complete a course of CBT achieve clinically meaningful improvement at follow-up, and SSRI treatment shows comparable response. Favourable prognostic factors include shorter illness duration, higher baseline insight, absence of comorbid personality disorder, and strong therapeutic alliance. Comorbid severe depression, delusional intensity of illness conviction, and high somatic symptom burden predict a more protracted course. Even in patients who do not achieve full remission, treatment consistently reduces medical utilisation, improves quality of life, and reduces distress to a manageable level.
recoveryTimeline: CBT response typically begins to emerge around sessions 4–6, with meaningful symptom reduction apparent by 12 weeks. A full course of 12–16 sessions over 4–5 months is usually required to consolidate gains and address relapse prevention. SSRI response builds more slowly: at least 8–12 weeks at a therapeutic dose is needed before efficacy can be assessed, and full benefit often continues to accrue over 6 months. Many patients benefit from 12 months or more of continued medication after response before a supervised taper. Relapse prevention through booster CBT sessions and maintenance medication where indicated is part of the long-term plan.
complications: Severe functional impairment — inability to work, socialise, or manage daily life — in the minority of patients with treatment-resistant chronic hypochondriasis.
Iatrogenic harm from repeated unnecessary investigations: radiation exposure from imaging, procedural complications, incidental findings generating further anxiety.
Major depression as a comorbid complication in 60–80% of chronic cases, worsening prognosis and increasing suicide risk.
Strained personal relationships and family burden as health-fear dominates household conversations and limits social participation.
Medically unexplained symptom escalation, where repeated reassurance paradoxically amplifies symptom reporting over time.
Delayed diagnosis of genuine medical conditions in people whose complaints are dismissed as health anxiety — proportionate, time-limited medical evaluation must remain part of care.

supportResources.name: NHS — Health Anxiety
supportResources.description: Plain-English patient information on health anxiety symptoms, cognitive maintenance, and treatment options available through NHS mental health services.

supportResources.name: NICE — Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder and Body Dysmorphic Disorder: Treatment (CG31)
supportResources.description: NICE clinical guideline that covers OCD-spectrum presentations including health anxiety, with stepped-care treatment recommendations.

supportResources.name: Anxiety and Depression Association of America (ADAA) — Illness Anxiety Disorder
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient-facing overview of illness anxiety disorder from the leading U.S. anxiety disorders advocacy organisation.

sources.title: Olatunji BO et al. Efficacy of Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy for Hypochondriasis: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Behaviour Research and Therapy, 2014
NICE Clinical Guideline CG31 — Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder and Body Dysmorphic Disorder
Fink P et al. The Prevalence of Somatoform Disorders Among Internal Medical Inpatients. Journal of Psychosomatic Research, 2004
Barsky AJ and Ahern DK. Cognitive Behavior Therapy for Hypochondriasis. JAMA, 2004
Hedman E et al. Internet-Based Cognitive Behaviour Therapy for Severe Health Anxiety: Randomised Controlled Trial. BMJ, 2011

NHS England — Mental Health: Health Anxiety Overview

confusedWithConditions.name: Obsessive-compulsive disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: OCD involves intrusive obsessions and compulsions across multiple themes; health anxiety in hypochondriasis is specifically focused on illness belief. The disorders overlap and often co-occur, but OCD is diagnosed when multiple obsessional themes are present.

confusedWithConditions.name: Generalised anxiety disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: GAD involves worry across multiple domains of life — finances, relationships, work — whereas hypochondriasis worry is specifically and persistently centred on having serious illness.

confusedWithConditions.name: Somatisation disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Somatisation disorder is defined by multiple unexplained physical symptoms across different body systems; hypochondriasis is defined by fear of illness despite absence or amplification of symptoms.

confusedWithConditions.name: Depression
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Depression can produce somatic preoccupation and health worry, but the core features are persistent low mood, anhedonia, and neurovegetative changes; hypochondriasis persists even when mood is not depressed.

relatedConditions.name: Somatisation disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Both fall under ICD-10 somatoform disorders and involve physical preoccupation; differential diagnosis shapes different treatment targets.

relatedConditions.name: Obsessive-compulsive disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares cognitive mechanisms of intrusive thoughts and compulsive checking; many patients benefit from OCD-specific CBT with ERP protocols.

relatedConditions.name: Post-traumatic stress disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Traumatic medical experiences or illness-related trauma can trigger or maintain health anxiety as part of a broader PTSD presentation.

relatedConditions.name: Generalised anxiety disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently co-occurs with hypochondriasis; treatment of GAD often reduces health anxiety as well.

faqs.question: What is hypochondriasis?
faqs.answer: Hypochondriasis is a recognised mental health condition (ICD-10: F45.21) in which a person persistently fears having a serious illness despite normal test results and medical reassurance. It is driven by misinterpreting normal body sensations as dangerous, and affects 1–5% of adults.

faqs.question: Is hypochondriasis the same as health anxiety?
faqs.answer: The terms are used interchangeably in clinical practice. ICD-10 uses 'hypochondriacal disorder'; DSM-5 replaced it with 'illness anxiety disorder' and 'somatic symptom disorder'. Health anxiety is the preferred patient-friendly term, referring to the same persistent, distressing preoccupation with having serious illness.

faqs.question: Can hypochondriasis be cured?
faqs.answer: Many people achieve substantial or complete remission with cognitive-behavioural therapy, SSRI medication, or both. About 50–65% of patients who complete CBT reach clinically meaningful improvement maintained at one year. Even those who do not fully remit typically experience significant reduction in distress and health-care utilisation.

faqs.question: What causes health anxiety?
faqs.answer: Health anxiety arises from a combination of a biological tendency toward anxiety, learned illness-focused beliefs from childhood, past experience of serious illness in self or family, and cognitive habits of interpreting body sensations catastrophically. Reassurance-seeking behaviours maintain the cycle once established.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for hypochondriasis?
faqs.answer: Cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT) is the gold-standard first-line treatment, with 50–65% response rates in randomised trials. SSRI antidepressants are effective especially when depression or OCD features are present. Combined CBT and medication is used for severe or treatment-resistant cases. NICE recommends a stepped-care approach beginning with self-help CBT.

faqs.question: Does CBT work for health anxiety?
faqs.answer: Yes. CBT for health anxiety has the strongest evidence base of any treatment, with multiple randomised controlled trials and meta-analyses confirming clinically significant improvement in 50–65% of completers. Internet-delivered CBT has also been shown effective. Response builds over 12–16 sessions and is maintained at follow-up.

faqs.question: Is hypochondriasis related to OCD?
faqs.answer: Hypochondriasis shares important features with OCD: intrusive health-fear thoughts function like obsessions, and checking and reassurance-seeking function like compulsions. Many experts regard it as part of the OCD spectrum, and CBT with exposure and response prevention, the gold-standard OCD treatment, is one of the most effective approaches.

faqs.question: Can antidepressants help with health anxiety?
faqs.answer: Yes. SSRIs such as fluoxetine, paroxetine, and escitalopram have demonstrated efficacy in randomised trials for hypochondriasis, with response rates of 60–70% in some studies. They are particularly indicated when depression or OCD features are present. A minimum trial of 8–12 weeks at a therapeutic dose is needed to assess response.

faqs.question: Why does reassurance from a doctor not help?
faqs.answer: Medical reassurance provides brief relief but maintains health anxiety long-term because it reinforces the belief that checking is necessary and prevents extinction of the underlying fear. Once the anxiety habituates — without receiving reassurance — the fear diminishes. CBT specifically targets this reassurance cycle as a maintaining behaviour.

faqs.question: How is hypochondriasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires at least 6 months of persistent illness preoccupation causing distress or impairment, persisting despite normal medical evaluation. A psychiatrist or psychologist uses the clinical interview, validated tools such as the Health Anxiety Inventory, and systematic exclusion of genuine medical conditions and other psychiatric diagnoses.

faqs.question: Is hypochondriasis more common in men or women?
faqs.answer: Unlike most anxiety disorders, hypochondriasis affects men and women in roughly equal proportions. Peak onset occurs in early adulthood in the 20s and 30s, with a second incidence peak around the 50s in both sexes.

faqs.question: Can hypochondriasis lead to depression?
faqs.answer: Yes. Major depression co-occurs in 60–80% of people with chronic hypochondriasis. The persistent distress, functional impairment, and sense of being misunderstood or dismissed by medical professionals can drive low mood, hopelessness, and, in severe cases, suicidal ideation. Both conditions require treatment.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hypochondriasis and somatic symptom disorder?
faqs.answer: In hypochondriasis the primary feature is fear of having illness, even with minimal or no physical symptoms. In somatic symptom disorder the person experiences prominent, distressing physical symptoms with disproportionate thoughts, feelings, or behaviours about them. The distinction influences treatment focus but both respond to CBT.

faqs.question: How long does hypochondriasis last?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, hypochondriasis typically follows a chronic waxing-and-waning course; 50–70% of untreated patients still meet criteria at five-year follow-up. With evidence-based treatment, the majority of patients achieve sustained improvement. Shorter illness duration before treatment predicts better outcome.

faqs.question: Does stress make health anxiety worse?
faqs.answer: Yes. Acute stressors reduce general psychological resilience and increase physiological arousal, providing more body sensations to misinterpret. Bereavement, job loss, and relationship breakdown are recognised precipitants of hypochondriasis onset and relapse. Stress management skills are an important component of CBT for health anxiety.

faqs.question: Can I self-help with health anxiety?
faqs.answer: Structured self-help using CBT-based workbooks, such as those recommended by NICE for stepped care, is an evidence-based first step. Apps delivering CBT exercises for health anxiety also have growing trial support. Self-help is most effective for mild to moderate cases and as a complement to professional therapy for severe presentations.

faqs.question: What should I tell my family about my health anxiety?
faqs.answer: Explaining that health anxiety is a recognised mental health condition that is maintained by reassurance helps family members understand why providing constant reassurance is unhelpful, however well-intentioned. Asking them to respond with brief acknowledgement rather than detailed reassurance reduces one of the key maintaining factors outside therapy sessions.

faqs.question: Is internet searching making my health anxiety worse?
faqs.answer: Almost always, yes. 'Cyberchondria' — compulsive health searching online — follows the same reassurance cycle as clinic visits: brief relief followed by increased anxiety, and inevitably finding descriptions of serious conditions that match normal sensations. CBT specifically targets limiting these searches as a core behavioural intervention.

faqs.question: Can hypochondriasis affect physical health?
faqs.answer: Chronic health anxiety is associated with genuinely worse physical health outcomes through mechanisms including avoidance of exercise (fearing it will reveal illness), sleep disruption, and the physiological effects of sustained stress. Paradoxically, the fear of illness can impair the behaviours, such as healthy activity and regular preventive care, that maintain genuine health.

faqs.question: When should I see a psychiatrist for health anxiety?
faqs.answer: See a psychiatrist if health anxiety has persisted for six months or more, is significantly impairing daily life, has not responded to self-help or brief primary-care support, or is accompanied by depression, suicidal thoughts, or severe functional decline. Earlier referral leads to better long-term outcomes and reduces unnecessary medical investigations.

metaDescription: Hypochondriasis is persistent illness fear despite normal test results. Learn about CBT and SSRI treatments, causes, and how to break the reassurance cycle.
